**Rawiri Wikuki Waititi** (born 17 October 1980 or 1981)[[1]](#cite_note-policy.nz_2020-1) is a New Zealand politician and [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") leader. He has been co-leader of [Te Pāti Māori](/wiki/Te_P%C4%81ti_M%C4%81ori "Te Pāti Māori") since 2020, alongside [Debbie Ngarewa-Packer](/wiki/Debbie_Ngarewa-Packer "Debbie Ngarewa-Packer"). He has served as the [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_parliament "Member of parliament") (MP) for [Waiariki](/wiki/Waiariki_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Waiariki (New Zealand electorate)") since [2020](/wiki/2020_New_Zealand_general_election "2020 New Zealand general election"),[[2]](#cite_note-40_new_MPs-2) when his election returned Te Pāti Māori to the [New Zealand Parliament](/wiki/New_Zealand_Parliament "New Zealand Parliament") following their defeat at the [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_New_Zealand_general_election "2017 New Zealand general election").

Born and raised in the eastern [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_Region "Bay of Plenty Region"), Waititi traces his lineage to many [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") but has firm links to [Te Whānau-ā-Apanui](/wiki/Te_Wh%C4%81nau-%C4%81-Apanui "Te Whānau-ā-Apanui") and [Ngāti Porou](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Porou "Ngāti Porou"). An advocate of [progressive](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism") political policies,[[3]](#cite_note-3) Waititi is a fluent [Māori speaker](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"), and is also an [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") leader, [Ringatū](/wiki/Ringat%C5%AB "Ringatū") minister, and [kapa haka](/wiki/Kapa_haka "Kapa haka") exponent.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He has been prominent and vocal in his opposition to the policies towards Māori of the [Sixth National Government](/wiki/Sixth_National_Government_of_New_Zealand "Sixth National Government of New Zealand") of New Zealand since the [2023 general election](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_general_election "2023 New Zealand general election").

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Waititi was born in [Ōpōtiki](/wiki/%C5%8Cp%C5%8Dtiki "Ōpōtiki"),[[5]](#cite_note-E-Tangata-5) the eldest of four children. His birthday is 17 October.[[6]](#cite_note-6) He spent his first 12 years living in Whangaparāoa, in the eastern [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty "Bay of Plenty") near [Cape Runaway](/wiki/Cape_Runaway "Cape Runaway"), and was schooled under the guidance of his kaumātua (elders) and his [hapū](/wiki/Hap%C5%AB "Hapū"), Te Whānau a Kauaetangohia. There he went to [kōhanga reo](/wiki/K%C5%8Dhanga_reo "Kōhanga reo") and Te Kura Mana Māori o Whangaparāoa, before he moved to [West Auckland](/wiki/West_Auckland,_New_Zealand "West Auckland, New Zealand") when he was 13[[5]](#cite_note-E-Tangata-5) to live with his paternal aunt, [June Mariu](/wiki/June_Mariu "June Mariu"), in [Te Atatū North (now Te Atatū Peninsula)](/wiki/Te_Atat%C5%AB_Peninsula "Te Atatū Peninsula"). He did his secondary schooling at [Rutherford High School](/wiki/Rutherford_College,_Auckland "Rutherford College, Auckland")[[5]](#cite_note-E-Tangata-5) (now Rutherford College) alongside another politician, [Simon Bridges](/wiki/Simon_Bridges "Simon Bridges").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Waititi is of the [Te Whānau-ā-Apanui](/wiki/Te_Wh%C4%81nau_a_Apanui "Te Whānau a Apanui"), [Ngāi Tai](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_Tai "Ngāi Tai"), [Te Whakatōhea](/wiki/Te_Whakat%C5%8Dhea "Te Whakatōhea"), [Ngāi Tūhoe](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_T%C5%ABhoe "Ngāi Tūhoe"), [Ngāti Awa](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Awa "Ngāti Awa"), [Te Arawa](/wiki/Te_Arawa "Te Arawa"), [Ngāti Tūwharetoa](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_T%C5%ABwharetoa "Ngāti Tūwharetoa"), [Ngāi Te Rangi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_Te_Rangi "Ngāi Te Rangi") and [Ngāti Ranginui](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Ranginui "Ngāti Ranginui") [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi"), and also has "firm links" to [Ngāti Porou](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Porou "Ngāti Porou").[[7]](#cite_note-7) He is a father of five[[8]](#cite_note-8) and husband to Kiri Tamihere-Waititi, the daughter of [John Tamihere](/wiki/John_Tamihere "John Tamihere").[[5]](#cite_note-E-Tangata-5) He is the grandnephew of [Hoani Waititi](/wiki/Hoani_Waititi "Hoani Waititi").[[9]](#cite_note-rotorua-9)

## Political career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political career")]

### Labour Party, 2014–2016

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Labour Party, 2014–2016")]

In the [2014 election](/wiki/2014_New_Zealand_general_election "2014 New Zealand general election"), Waititi ran for the [Labour Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_Labour_Party "New Zealand Labour Party") in Waiariki.[[9]](#cite_note-rotorua-9) As he was not placed on the Labour Party list, his only way to Parliament was to win Waiariki, however, he lost the seat of Waiariki to [Māori Party](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Party "Māori Party") co-leader [Te Ururoa Flavell](/wiki/Te_Ururoa_Flavell "Te Ururoa Flavell").[[10]](#cite_note-2014-election-10)

### Defection to the Māori Party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Defection to the Māori Party")]

In 2016, following [Kīngi Tūheitia Paki](/wiki/T%C5%ABheitia_Paki "Tūheitia Paki")'s speech backing the Māori Party,[[11]](#cite_note-11) Waititi announced he would be supporting the Māori Party.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### 2020 general election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2020 general election")]

On 23 February 2020, Waititi was announced as the Māori Party candidate for Waiariki for the [2020 election](/wiki/2020_New_Zealand_general_election "2020 New Zealand general election"). Following his nomination, Waititi said that there was "an imminent need, now more than ever that Māori have a voice who solely prioritises their aspirations and their needs and that is unapologetic about doing so. The Māori Party is the only party who can do that."[[13]](#cite_note-13) He was endorsed by Te Kapa Haka o Te Whānau a Apanui.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

At the 2020 election, Waititi successfully unseated the Labour MP [Tāmati Coffey](/wiki/T%C4%81mati_Coffey "Tāmati Coffey"), winning by 836 votes, and became the MP for Waiariki.[[15]](#cite_note-2020-election-15)

The final election results showed that the Māori Party had won 1.2% of the party vote, entitling them to two seats, so Waititi's electorate win meant not only his entry to Parliament, but also that of female co-leader [Debbie Ngarewa-Packer](/wiki/Debbie_Ngarewa-Packer "Debbie Ngarewa-Packer").[[16]](#cite_note-results2020-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) Of the forty-two new MPs elected to the [53rd Parliament](/wiki/53rd_New_Zealand_Parliament "53rd New Zealand Parliament"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) two are from the Māori Party.[[2]](#cite_note-40_new_MPs-2)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Under the Māori Party's constitution, its co-leaders must be drawn from its MPs first, with one male and one female co-leader.[[21]](#cite_note-21) At a special general meeting of the party on 28 October 2020, Waititi was confirmed as the male co-leader, replacing his father-in-law, [John Tamihere](/wiki/John_Tamihere "John Tamihere").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### First term, 2020–2023

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: First term, 2020–2023")]

Before being sworn in to the 53rd parliament, Waititi performed a waerea to protest being required to pledge allegiance to [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") without reference to the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi").[[23]](#cite_note-23) On 26 November, Waititi and Ngarewa-Packer walked out of Parliament after the [Speaker of the House](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_New_Zealand_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the New Zealand House of Representatives") [Trevor Mallard](/wiki/Trevor_Mallard "Trevor Mallard") declined his motion that the Māori Party be allowed to speak for 15 minutes during the opening on Parliament on the grounds that MPs from smaller parties were not scheduled to deliver their maiden speeches until the following week. Waititi described Mallard's decision and the parliamentary system as unfair.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In late December 2020 and early January, Waititi participated in negotiations with 16 prisoners who were involved in [unrest](/wiki/Waikeria_prison_unrest "Waikeria prison unrest") at [Waikeria Prison](/wiki/List_of_prisons_in_New_Zealand#Waikeria_Prison "List of prisons in New Zealand") stemming from allegations of inhumane and unhygienic conditions at the prison. Several of the prisoners had requested the presence of a Māori leader such as Waititi as a prerequisite to ending the unrest. He stated, "these men belong to [whanau](/wiki/Whanau "Whanau")... that they deserve the right to be treated humanely, with fresh water, food and clean clothing and they deserve to have someone advocating for them."[[25]](#cite_note-25) Following five days of unrest, the prisoners surrendered to the authorities following negotiations involving Waititi.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

On 9 February, Waititi was ejected from the debating chamber by Speaker Mallard for refusing to wear a [necktie](/wiki/Necktie "Necktie") in line with Parliament's business attire dress core. Waititi instead wore a [hei tiki](/wiki/Hei-tiki "Hei-tiki") necklace, which he described as Māori business attire. Waititi had earlier criticised wearing neckties, describing them as "colonial noose[s]" during his maiden speech last year. When Waititi attempted to ask [Corrections Minister](/wiki/Minister_of_Corrections_(New_Zealand) "Minister of Corrections (New Zealand)") [Kelvin Davis](/wiki/Kelvin_Davis_(politician) "Kelvin Davis (politician)") a supplementary question, Mallard denied him permission to speak since he was not wearing a tie. When Waititi sought a [point of order](/wiki/Point_of_order "Point of order"), Mallard ordered him to leave. Waititi was supported by fellow Māori Party MP Ngarewa-Packer, who wore a tie in mockery of the rules.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) The following day, a Standing Orders meeting accepted a Māori Party submission proposing the elimination of neckties from Parliament's business attire. As a result, Mallard announced that it would no longer be compulsory to wear ties in the chamber.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

On 12 May, Waititi was ejected from parliamentary proceedings following a heated argument with the opposition [National Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party") leader [Judith Collins](/wiki/Judith_Collins "Judith Collins") about the proposed creation of a [Māori Health Authority](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Health_Authority "Māori Health Authority"). In the past two weeks, National had alleged the Labour Government was promoting a "separatist agenda" through the Māori Health Authority and other policies seeking to fulfil partnership responsibilities under the Treaty of Waitangi. Waititi accused Collins of racism and sought to raise a point of order about indigenous rights. When his point of order was denied by the Speaker Mallard, Waititi performed a [haka](/wiki/Haka "Haka") in protest, prompting the Speaker to order him to leave Parliament. Waititi left with Māori Party co-leader Ngarewa-Packer and Green MP [Teanau Tuiono](/wiki/Teanau_Tuiono "Teanau Tuiono"), who expressed solidarity with him.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In October 2021, Waititi criticised the Government's abandonment of its previous COVID-19 elimination strategy and expressed concerns that the new [COVID-19 Protection Framework](/wiki/COVID-19_Protection_Framework "COVID-19 Protection Framework") was insufficient in protecting Māori and boosting the Māori vaccination rate.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In September 2022, Waititi and fellow Māori Party MP Ngarewa-Packer voted against the [Queen Elizabeth II Memorial Day Act 2022](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II_Memorial_Day_Act_2022 "Queen Elizabeth II Memorial Day Act 2022"), which created a once-off public holiday on 26 September to commemorate the [death of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Elizabeth_II "Death and state funeral of Elizabeth II"). Waititi objected to the holiday on the grounds that no similar event had taken place for any deceased Māori leaders and claimed it was "example of [colonialism](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") in practice". Waititi further stated after a week of avoiding commenting on [republicanism](/wiki/Republicanism_in_New_Zealand "Republicanism in New Zealand") that New Zealand "must acknowledge the brutal genocidal and ongoing impact of colonialism, of the imperial project that was overseen by the [House of Windsor](/wiki/House_of_Windsor "House of Windsor") and its forebears". Waititi's remarks were criticised as insensitive and disrespectful by [National Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party") MPs [Michael Woodhouse](/wiki/Michael_Woodhouse "Michael Woodhouse") and Judith Collins.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In May 2023, Waititi and Ngarewa-Packer were ordered to leave Parliament by Speaker [Adrian Rurawhe](/wiki/Adrian_Rurawhe "Adrian Rurawhe") after they staged a [haka](/wiki/Haka "Haka") (dance) to welcome former Labour MP [Meka Whaitiri](/wiki/Meka_Whaitiri "Meka Whaitiri"). The Māori Party caucus had not sought permission from the Speaker or other parties to hold the haka.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In mid July 2023, Waititi joked about poisoning [ACT Party](/wiki/ACT_New_Zealand "ACT New Zealand") leader [David Seymour](/wiki/David_Seymour_(New_Zealand_politician) "David Seymour (New Zealand politician)") during Te Pati Māori's annual conference. While referring to the [karaka](/wiki/Karaka_(tree) "Karaka (tree)") seedpod necklace around his neck, Waititi said: "These are karaka berries and they've still got the poison in them. So next time I go into Parliament this is what I'm going to do. When David Seymour's not looking, I'm going to go like this into his water... There you are, re-indigenise yourself with some native seeds." Waititi's remarks were condemned by Seymour, who demanded an apology.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

On 29 August 2023, Waititi was suspended from Parliament for 24 hours after referring to suppressed court proceedings while asking a question during parliamentary proceedings. Though Waititi asked the question under [parliamentary privilege](/wiki/Parliamentary_privilege "Parliamentary privilege"), his reference to the court proceedings breached two parliamentary Standing Orders. In addition, Speaker Rurawhe referred a "general question" of breaching court suppressions to Parliament's Privileges Committee.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) As punishment, Waititi was barred from voting, sitting on a committee or entering the debating chamber for 24 hours. On 28 August 2024, Parliament's Privileges Committee chairperson Judith Collins upheld Speaker Rurawhe's disciplinary action against Waititi for breaching the court suppression order, stating "that free speech came with a responsibility not to frustrate the court's jurisdiction."[[42]](#cite_note-42)

During an interview with TVNZ journalist [Jack Tame](/wiki/Jack_Tame "Jack Tame") 10 September 2023, Waititi advocated a wealth tax and removing [GST](/wiki/Goods_and_Services_Tax_(New_Zealand) "Goods and Services Tax (New Zealand)") from food, defended [Te Aka Whai Ora](/wiki/Te_Aka_Whai_Ora "Te Aka Whai Ora") (the Māori Health Authority), and advocated a policy of [neutrality](/wiki/Neutral_country "Neutral country").[[43]](#cite_note-TVNZ_2023-43) Waititi also denied that his party's sports policy' comments about "Māori genetic makeup being stronger than others" were racist. These comments were subsequently deleted from Te Pāti Māori's website. When challenged by Tame, he said that Te Pāti Māori was "trying to empower people that are climbing out from the bottom of the bonnet of colonial violence for the last 193 years" by encouraging pride in their heritage.[[43]](#cite_note-TVNZ_2023-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Second term, 2023–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Second term, 2023–present")]

In the [2023 general election](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_general_election "2023 New Zealand general election"), Waititi again contested the Waiariki electorate. He received 21,500 votes out of 28,958 for an outright majority.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) Waititi performed a haka in the chamber prior to swearing his oath of allegiance to King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

In mid-December 2023, Waititi retained his position as Te Pāti Māori co-leader and joined Parliament's finance & expenditure select committee. He also became the party's finance, economic development, trade & enterprise, revenue, procurement, defence, foreign affairs, intelligence, Māori performance arts, and arts, culture & heritage spokesperson.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In mid-March 2024, Waititi introduced a member's bill to amend the Goods and Services Tax Act to remove the [Goods and Services Tax](/wiki/Goods_and_Services_Tax_(New_Zealand) "Goods and Services Tax (New Zealand)") from all food products and non-alcohol products.[[49]](#cite_note-49) The bill was defeated at its first reading on 22 March 2024, with only Te Pāti Māori supporting it.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

On 14 November 2024, Waititi along with Ngarewa-Packer and Labour MP [Peeni Henare](/wiki/Peeni_Henare "Peeni Henare") joined fellow Te Pāti Māori MP [Hana-Rawhiti Maipi-Clarke](/wiki/Hana-Rawhiti_Maipi-Clarke "Hana-Rawhiti Maipi-Clarke") in performing an impromptu protest [haka](/wiki/Haka "Haka"), which disrupted parliamentary proceedings during the first reading of the [Treaty Principles Bill](/wiki/Treaty_Principles_Bill "Treaty Principles Bill"). On 10 December, Waititi and the other three MPs were referred to Parliament's Privileges Committee for their involvement in the haka.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

On 1 April 2025, Waititi, Ngarewa-Packer and Maipi-Clark declined to appear before the Privileges Committee, claiming they had been denied key legal rights such as a joint hearing, restrictions on their legal representation [Christopher Finlayson](/wiki/Christopher_Finlayson "Christopher Finlayson"), expert testimony from [tikanga](/wiki/Tikanga "Tikanga") (Māori culture) expert Tā Pou Temara denied, hearing scheduling conflicts being ignored and concerns about disciplinary action against Maipi-Clarke.[[52]](#cite_note-52) On 2 April, the Privileges Committee's chairperson [Judith Collins](/wiki/Judith_Collins "Judith Collins") confirmed that the privileges hearing would go ahead regardless of whether the three TPM MPs turned up.[[53]](#cite_note-53) In response, Waititi and Ngarewa-Packer announced that Te Pāti Māori would boycott the hearing and hold its own "alternative independent hearing," dismissing the Privileges Committee as a "[kangaroo court](/wiki/Kangaroo_court "Kangaroo court")."[[54]](#cite_note-54)

On 14 May 2025, the Privileges Committee censured Waititi and his colleagues for "acting in a manner that could have the effect of intimidating a member of the House in the discharge of their duty" during the haka protest. He and Ngarewa-Packer were suspended from Parliament for 21 days while Maipi-Clark was suspended for a week. Te Pāti Māori issued a statement denouncing the verdict as "the worst punishment handed down ever in our history."[[55]](#cite_note-55) On 20 May 2025, Parliament adopted [Leader of the House](/wiki/Leader_of_the_House_(New_Zealand) "Leader of the House (New Zealand)") [Chris Bishop](/wiki/Chris_Bishop "Chris Bishop")'s motion that the parliamentary debate on the TPM MPs' suspension be delayed until 5 June, allowing them to participate in the [budget](/wiki/2025_New_Zealand_budget "2025 New Zealand budget") debate on 22 May.[[56]](#cite_note-56) On 5 June, parliament voted to suspend Maipi-Clark, Ngarewa-Packer and Waititi for 21 days.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

A RNZ-Reid Research poll found that, a majority supported the punishment or thought it should be stronger. 37.0% said it was "about right"; 17.2% said it was "too lenient", and 36.2% said it was "too harsh".[[58]](#cite_note-58)

## Views and positions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Views and positions")]

### Abortion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Abortion")]

Waititi voted in favour of the [Contraception, Sterilisation, and Abortion (Safe Areas) Amendment Act 2022](/wiki/Contraception,_Sterilisation,_and_Abortion_(Safe_Areas)_Amendment_Act_2022 "Contraception, Sterilisation, and Abortion (Safe Areas) Amendment Act 2022"), which established safe zones around abortion providers.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Conversion therapy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Conversion therapy")]

Waititi has supported the [Conversion Practices Prohibition Legislation Act 2022](/wiki/Conversion_Practices_Prohibition_Legislation_Act_2022 "Conversion Practices Prohibition Legislation Act 2022"), which banned [conversion therapy](/wiki/Conversion_therapy "Conversion therapy"). During the Bill's first reading in August 2022, he claimed that conversion therapy was based on European colonial ideas about gender and sexuality that were alien to [Māori people](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people").[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Russian invasion of Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Russian invasion of Ukraine")]

In March 2022, Waititi supported the [Russia Sanctions Act 2022](/wiki/Russia_Sanctions_Act_2022 "Russia Sanctions Act 2022"), which created an autonomous sanctions regime in response to the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"). While condemning the Russian invasion of Ukraine, he also questioned New Zealand's failure to condemn the United States' invasions of Afghanistan and Iraq, and the Israeli occupation of Palestine.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

He later called the conflict a "[proxy war](/wiki/Proxy_war "Proxy war") for the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")", stating that New Zealand should stay out of the conflict.[[62]](#cite_note-62) He said that New Zealand was acting like a [puppet state](/wiki/Puppet_state "Puppet state") of the United States, a statement that saw disagreement from then Prime Minister [Jacinda Ardern](/wiki/Jacinda_Ardern "Jacinda Ardern") and other political leaders across the aisle.[[63]](#cite_note-63) He supported a position of neutrality.[[64]](#cite_note-64) In a [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") post on 21 March 2023, he stated that New Zealand should be the "[Switzerland of the South Pacific](/wiki/Swiss_neutrality "Swiss neutrality")", saying (in reference to the conflict) that Māori should not fight "other indigenous peoples on their *whenua*."[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Self-governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rawiri_Waititi&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Self-governance")]

In February 2024, Waititi has advocated for Māori [self-governance](/wiki/Self_determination "Self determination"), stating that Māori have a right to govern themselves. He has also called for the establishment of a Māori Parliament.[[66]](#cite_note-66)
